purpose: Reference guide for generating InfographicTemplate JSON from a transcript. Use this alongside the transcript to pick layouts, keep segment text concise, and structure the video coherently.
globalRules: Use one clear idea per segment.
Use subtitles for the interpretation, not the same words as the title.
Use items for the supporting points.
Prefer 3 to 6 items unless the layout specifically suggests otherwise.
Use values only when the layout needs numeric emphasis or a data comparison.
Use videoShown true only when the original source video should play instead of an animated layout.
If intro is true, show the hookText as an overlay at the start of the video; do not add extra duration for it.

videoShown.useWhen: The source video should continue on screen for this section.

animatedSegment.useWhen: You want a designed infographic scene instead of the source video.
notes: Add only the fields that help the specific layout.

layouts.label: Flowchart
layouts.useWhen: The audience should follow a clear progression from one idea to the next.
layouts.notes: Use short action phrases for each step.

layouts.label: Bar graph
layouts.useWhen: You need to compare values visually and make the scale matter.
layouts.notes: Items are labels. Values should align with each label and represent the comparative magnitude.

layouts.label: Timeline
layouts.useWhen: The transcript mentions an order of events or a sequence across time.
layouts.notes: Write each item as a milestone or phase, not a paragraph.

layouts.label: Comparison
layouts.useWhen: You want the audience to compare two or more contrasting ideas side by side.
layouts.notes: Keep the compared items parallel in structure.

layouts.label: Radial map
layouts.useWhen: One central idea branches into several related points.
layouts.notes: Make the middle idea obvious and keep the surrounding items tightly related.

layouts.label: Matrix
layouts.useWhen: You need to organize ideas into a compact grid with four distinct zones.
layouts.notes: Use four clear labels. This works best when the transcript naturally divides into four buckets.

layouts.label: Stats stack
layouts.useWhen: A segment should land a few punchy metrics or evidence points instead of a process.
layouts.notes: Use short stat-style statements. The visual emphasis is on the numbers or the punchline.

layouts.label: Process
layouts.useWhen: The transcript explains how work moves through stages or how a system is improved.
layouts.notes: This is the best default for operational or procedural storytelling.

layouts.label: Pyramid
layouts.useWhen: The message should move from a wide base of support to a focused top point.
layouts.notes: Use lower levels for broad drivers and upper levels for the final takeaway.

layouts.label: Roadmap
layouts.useWhen: The transcript points to a future path, implementation plan, or growth sequence.
layouts.notes: Think of this as a future-facing timeline with a stronger sense of direction.

layouts.label: Hierarchy
layouts.useWhen: The content should be shown as a top-down structure with parent and child relationships.
layouts.notes: Use when the transcript describes structure, ownership, or levels of control.

layouts.label: Quadrant
layouts.useWhen: The idea naturally belongs in a four-box grid and each box needs a distinct label.
layouts.notes: This is ideal for concise classification or strategic framing.

promptingNotes: Choose layouts based on the meaning of the transcript, not on visual variety alone.
If a transcript section is instructional, process and roadmap are usually the first layouts to consider.
If a section is comparative, choose comparison, barGraph, matrix, or quadrant.
If a section is descriptive but compact, use stats, pyramid, radial, or hierarchy.
When in doubt, keep the text short and let the layout carry the structure.
